elements.text: All three are very interesting!

text: First time hearing about SNARC! fascinating. We count on our hands left to right too.

text:
Thanks for sharing <@UCUSW7WVD> :slightly_smiling_face: If anyone's curious, happy to chat through the process or like where this all came from.

I've been spending a lot of time thinking about what kind of new roles might pop up if things keep progressing the way they have been. This series came about as I had started asking Claude to write some Sci Fi about a particular role we came up with in a chat and some of it ended up really good, so I decided I'd try to see how far I could push it and polish it up enough to feel good about publishing.

text: I think one unrealistic thing here is when he already understands what Carol wants. At least in my urban milieu I find nobody cares enough about anyone else's inner life to _that_ extent. Mostly we dodder along. But yeah, reasonable fiction to introduce to increase contrast.

text: Huh interesting, yeah I can remember that feeling when I lived in New York. Maybe I've spent too long in the suburbs these last few years that I've gotten softer haha...when I moved out here I remember a bunch of the service people I worked with made comments about understanding me being a bit more high strung being a new homeowner and doing a little bit extra because of it

text:
Living in a small city in a very heavily agricultural area, and having grown up in a small town — the small town, small-time farming, and serving people one knows personally in your story really resonated. Even here, though, small family farms aren't the norm anymore, I'm afraid.

In fact, if LLMs somehow could help us regain the "smallness" of technology rather than the big-tech we have today, I'd be a pretty big fan. Not sure I can share your optimism in good conscience, but I'm sure willing to strive for it!

text: Working on it <@U04E5QAD6DD>.  I've taken up the saying, "If you can't dream it, you can't be it."  We need dreams of small tech, and I think LLMs can help.

.  I've taken up the saying, "If you can't dream it, you can't be it."  We need dreams of small tech, and I think LLMs can help.

text: Oh, weird. I’ve never heard of snarc before - thanks for mentioning it! A few moments bouncing this off of an LLM (Perplexity in this case) results in: Forth and APL syntax is preferred over Lisp syntax, both are preferred over infix. Data on which side of the road to drive on totally contradicts snarc prediction. Snarc does not seem to be a big enough factor in our (western) cultural choice of representing numbers with most significant digits on the left. I read Raskin’s “Humane Interface” a long time ago. Apparently he mentions Fitt’s and Hick’s laws in chapter 4. Snarc was discovered after Raskin’s book. Apparently, snarc validates Raskin’s claims.

text: further: my gut feel and experience says that programming notation and PLs need to be characterized along lines of 1. exploration, 2. development, 3. production engineering, 4, maintenance.

text: Cognitive HCI theories go beyond this, if you're willing to admit things that aren't behavioral "laws" with well-defined numbers. For example, <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_foraging|Information Foraging> describes how people engage with graph-like information environments and is pretty well-established and has been found to apply in many contexts (reading code, browsing the web, skimming search engine results, searching wikis, etc.). This gives an answer to questions like "Why is it helpful that Google's search results page includes excerpts from the pages?" The answer is that it helps provide a stronger "information scent".

elements.text: Cognitive HCI theories go beyond this, if you're willing to admit things that aren't behavioral "laws" with well-defined numbers. For example,
Information Foraging

describes how people engage with graph-like information environments and is pretty well-established and has been found to apply in many contexts (reading code, browsing the web, skimming search engine results, searching wikis, etc.). This gives an answer to questions like "Why is it helpful that Google's search results page includes excerpts from the pages?" The answer is that it helps provide a stronger "information scent".

text: In the data visualization world, there's the <https://www.cs.umd.edu/~ben/papers/Shneiderman1996eyes.pdf|Visual Information-Seeking Mantra> ("Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand"), which is a good guide for many different applications across domains (data dashboards, google maps, really any interactive data viz)
